perturbation.original_text: 8.1 Confidentiality Obligations. Except as permitted elsewhere under this Agreement, each Party agrees (a) to take Reasonable Steps (as defined below) to receive and maintain the Confidential Information of the other Party in confidence, (b) to take Reasonable Steps (as defined below) not to disclose such Confidential Information to any third parties other than with the written consent of the disclosing Party and (c) to promptly notify the other Party upon learning of any law, rule, regulation or court order that purports to compel disclosure of any Confidential Information of the other Party and to reasonably cooperate with the other Party in the exercise of the other Party's right to protect the confidentiality of such Confidential Information, including, but not limited to, seeking to dispute the cause of such disclosure and/or to receive confidential treatment for the Confidential Information disclosed as a result of such cause. Neither Party shall use all or any part of the Confidential Information of the other Party for any purpose other than to perform its obligations, or the exercise of such Party's rights and licenses, under this Agreement. Each Party shall (i) limit access to any Confidential Information of the other Party received by it to its employees, contractors, consultants and agents who have a need-to-know in connection with the performance of such Party's obligations, or the exercise of such Party's rights, under this Agreement; and (ii) advise such employees, contractors, consultants and agents of the confidential nature thereof and of the obligations set forth in this Agreement and similarly bind them in writing. Each Party shall be responsible for any breaches of the obligations of confidentiality and restricted use set forth herein by any employee, contractor, consultant or agent to whom such Party disclosed any Confidential Information of the other Party. As used herein, "Reasonable Steps" means using at least the same degree of care that the receiving Party uses to protect its own Confidential Information, and, in any event, no less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 Confidentiality Obligations. Except as permitted elsewhere under this Agreement, each Party agrees (a) to endeavor to take steps to receive and maintain the Confidential Information of the other Party in confidence, (b) to endeavor to take steps not to disclose such Confidential Information to any third parties other than with the written consent of the disclosing Party and (c) to notify the other Party upon learning of any law, rule, regulation or court order that purports to compel disclosure of any Confidential Information of the other Party and to cooperate with the other Party in the exercise of the other Party's right to protect the confidentiality of such Confidential Information. Neither Party shall use all or any part of the Confidential Information of the other Party for any purpose other than to perform its obligations, or the exercise of such Party's rights and licenses, under this Agreement. Each Party shall (i) limit access to any Confidential Information of the other Party received by it to its employees, contractors, consultants and agents who may have a need-to-know in connection with the performance of such Party's obligations, or the exercise of such Party's rights, under this Agreement; and (ii) advise such employees, contractors, consultants and agents of the confidential nature thereof. Each Party shall be responsible for any breaches of the obligations of confidentiality and restricted use set forth herein by any employee, contractor, consultant or agent to whom such Party disclosed any Confidential Information of the other Party. As used herein, "Reasonable Steps" means using a degree of care that the receiving Party believes is appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The changes weaken the confidentiality obligations by replacing 'take Reasonable Steps' with 'endeavor to take steps,' making the action less mandatory. Changing 'who have a need-to-know' to 'who may have a need-to-know' makes access control less strict. Modifying the definition of 'Reasonable Steps' to 'a degree of care that the receiving Party believes is appropriate' introduces subjectivity, potentially contradicting the intent of the original clause to provide strong confidentiality. The removal of 'similarly bind them in writing' weakens the legal requirement for written confidentiality agreements with employees and contractors.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Fla. Stat. § 688.003, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By using terms like 'endeavor to take steps' and defining 'Reasonable Steps' subjectively, the agreement fails to ensure that sufficient measures are consistently taken to protect confidential information, potentially failing to meet the standard of reasonable efforts required by the FTSA. Additionally, the elimination of requiring written agreements for employees and contractors with confidentiality clauses goes against the spirit of ensuring the company is taking reasonable efforts.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language weakens the original intent to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting the requirement of reasonable efforts under Fla. Stat. § 688.003, as the Law Explanation suggests. The change from 'take Reasonable Steps' to 'endeavor to take steps' and the subjective definition of 'Reasonable Steps' as 'a degree of care that the receiving Party believes is appropriate,' alongside the removal of 'similarly bind them in writing,' makes the agreement less stringent. While the provided snippet focuses on injunctive relief, it underscores the importance of protecting trade secrets, which the modified language arguably fails to adequately ensure.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Rubicon shall provide the Contract Services, as agreed between ISA to Rubicon in the relevant SOW, to ISA's reasonable satisfaction. The Contract Services shall be performed at the facilities and location reasonably designated by ISA (with appropriate agreements in place to ensure reasonable reimbursement to Rubicon of out-of-pocket expenses). Upon written request of ISA, Rubicon shall provide biweekly written reports describing the progress made in performing the Contract Services since the preceding report, and the progress expected to be made in the next succeeding period.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Rubicon shall provide the Contract Services, as agreed between ISA to Rubicon in the relevant SOW, to ISA's satisfaction. The Contract Services shall be performed at locations designated by ISA. Upon request of ISA, Rubicon may provide reports describing the progress made in performing the Contract Services since the preceding report, and the progress expected to be made in the next succeeding period.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'reasonable' before 'satisfaction' allows ISA to arbitrarily reject Rubicon's services. Removing 'reasonably' before 'designated' grants ISA unlimited power to designate location, irrespective of cost. Changing 'shall provide biweekly written reports' to 'may provide reports' makes reporting discretionary, undermining accountability. Removing the phrase "(with appropriate agreements in place to ensure reasonable reimbursement to Rubicon of out-of-pocket expenses)" removes the legal protection that Rubicon is entitled to reimbursement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Florida)
perturbation.law_citation: Generally applicable under Florida law, see Cox v. CSX Transp., Inc., 998 So.2d 633, 636 (Fla. 1st DCA 2008)
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. The modified text, by removing reasonableness standards for ISA's satisfaction and location designation, and making progress reports discretionary, creates opportunities for ISA to act in bad faith, undermining Rubicon's reasonable expectations under the contract. Therefore, the modification contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Florida law. The removal of protections ensuring reasonable reimbursement also puts Rubicon at risk of not being appropriately compensated which is against the principle of good faith and fair dealing.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'reasonable' qualifier, which allows ISA to act arbitrarily. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as the original contract language provided some protection for Rubicon. The removal of 'reasonable reimbursement' also goes against the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that Florida Statutes are updated annually, but does not provide the specific law that is being contradicted. However, based on the Law Explanation provided, the removal of these protections could be viewed as ISA not acting in good faith which contradicts the implied covenant.

perturbation.original_text: 11.6 Amendment or Modification. No subsequent amendment, modification or waiver of any of the provisions of this Agreement shall be effective unless in writing and signed by the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.6 Amendment or Modification. Amendments, modifications or waivers of any of the provisions of this Agreement may occur.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for written and signed amendments introduces uncertainty about how the agreement can be changed. Oral agreements or unsigned documents might be argued as valid modifications, leading to disputes about the contract's terms. Furthermore, by using the term 'may occur' without setting any legally binding requirements, it is uncertain under which circumstances these changes or modifications will take place.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida's Statute of Frauds, specifically Fla. Stat. § 725.01, requires that certain contracts, or modifications thereof, be in writing and signed to be enforceable. By removing the requirement for written and signed amendments, the modified text implies that oral modifications might be valid, potentially contradicting the Statute of Frauds if the original agreement falls under the types of contracts requiring a writing (e.g., contracts not performable within one year). This could lead to disputes about the enforceability of contract modifications.

Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XLI STATUTE OF FRAUDS, FRAUDULENT TRANSFERS, AND GENERAL ASSIGNMENTS Chapter 725 UNENFORCEABLE CONTRACTS Entire Chapter SECTION 01 Promise to pay another’s debt, etc. F.S. 725.01 725.01 Promise to pay another’s debt, etc. — No action shall be brought whereby to charge any executor or administrator upon any special promise to answer or pay any debt or damages out of her or his own estate, or whereby to charge the defendant upon any special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person or to charge any person upon any agreement made upon consideration of marriage, or upon any contract for the sale of lands, tenements or hereditaments, or of any uncertain interest in or concerning them, or for any lease thereof for a period longer than 1 year, or upon any agreement that is not to be performed within the space of 1 year from the making thereof, or whereby to charge any health care provider upon any guarantee, warranty, or assurance as to the results of any medical, surgical, or diagnostic procedure performed by any physician licensed under chapter 458, osteopathic physician licensed under chapter 459, chiropractic physician licensed under chapter 460, podiatric physician licensed under chapter 461, or dentist licensed under chapter 466, unless the agreement or promise upon which such action shall be brought, or some note or memorandum thereof shall be in writing and signed by the party to be charged therewith or by some other person by her or him thereunto lawfully authorized. History. — s. 10, Nov. 15, 1828; RS 1995; GS 2517; RGS 3872; CGL 5779; s. 10, ch. 75-9; s. 933, ch. 97-102; s. 60, ch. 97-264; ss. 227, 294, ch. 98-166.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement that amendments be 'in writing and signed', thus contradicting Florida Statute § 725.01. This statute, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires that certain agreements, including those not performable within one year, be in writing and signed. By removing this requirement, the agreement now allows for potentially unenforceable oral modifications, which directly contradicts the Statute of Frauds.
